podcasts.name: Moody's Podcasts
podcasts.title: Growth & Strategy 101 | Back to basics
podcasts.excerpt: Hitting growth targets is often treated as a pure success metric, but missing them can create far wider risks for an organization. This episode reframes growth through a risk lens, exploring how revenue targets connect to confidence, culture, and long‑term performance. James Hargreave , Strategist for Growth and Strategy at Moody’s, joins host, Alex Pillow, to unpack why “missing your number” can be the most significant risk in business and how smarter strategy can reduce that risk. Together, they explore how data‑led GTM strategy, clearer targeting, and better enablement help organizations grow more confidently and sustainably. Key topics discussed: Why “missing your number” is a business risk that goes beyond revenue alone The importance of a strong Ideal Customer Profile (ICP) in focusing sales and marketing on winnable deals The role of data quality and connected systems in modern go‑to‑market strategy How CRM ecosystems and AI improve relevance, efficiency, and sales enablement Why optimizing go‑to‑market shifts where and how companies compete and differentiate Additional resources: Moody’s Sales and Marketing landing page Growth and Strategy ebook Growth and strategy blogs and case studies Steve Kleinmann and Ian Godfrey LinkedIn profiles Salesforce events Hosted by Simplecast, an AdsWizz company. See pcm.adswizz.com for information about our collection and use of personal data for advertising.
podcasts.transcript: By downloading or listening to this podcast, you are agreeing to Moody's legal terms and conditions found@moody's.com disclaimer, including that the information provided is not investment or financial advice and that Moody's will not be liable for losses arising from your use of the information. This is Risk Reframed, the show where we bring you the leading risk experts from Moody's, our partners and the wider ecosystem to discuss the risks organizations face, how they're interconnected, and ultimately what you, the risk professional, can do about them. As regular listeners will know, we moved to the Risk Reframe brand to widen our perspective on all types of risk. Hot on the heels of our discussions on third party risk management, trade, credit and supply chain risk management, today we're going to tackle arguably the biggest risk in business. Missing your number. Joining me is Jamie Hargreave, strategist for growth and strategy at Moody's. Jamie, how are you? I'm very well, thank you. Alex, how are you? Yeah, very well. It's always good when we get you down from Manchester. I know you don't love it down in London, but we force you down occasionally. Well, everybody talks about the weather, they pass me umbrellas, they talk about the rain. So coming down to London seems to go through the same sort of motions. I'm pretty like life up in Manchester at the moment. Good man. Growth and strategy is not something that typically risk management professionals will always think about. But tell me, is it the biggest risk in business? Yeah, it's an interesting way of framing things. I think when people think growth and strategy, they think about the positive nature of things. You know, growing the organization, hitting targets. But framing it as a risk to the business really leads to the second nature impacts of some of the things that can occur when you fail to hit your target. Obviously when you don't hit your sales number, you can see confidence drop within the organization, you can see investment start to fall in certain areas. And ultimately, if we look at the sort of listed market, you see things like stock prices dropping as well. So in terms of a risk to an organization, it might not be typically characterized as that, but the second order impacts of failing to hit your number can really be seen as a pretty significant risk to a business. Yeah, it's really good way of framing it and we'll unpack that a little bit. I mean, it did almost strike me as sort of a X KYC AML full timer. No, no sanctions, you know, affects, you know, geopolitics and human trafficking is one of the worst things I've ever had to like really look into for the for work. But it's matters so much to try and prevent it. However, if you are missing target, pressure increases. When pressure increases, people can sometimes take shortcuts which might then as you say have second or third order effects that can affect these things as well. So we always talk about connected risk. It is connected though it's a cliche for a reason at this point. Absolutely. We talked about investment dropping. If you can't invest in new technology to support things like modern slavery practices, then you're ultimately potentially going to fall foul in some of those areas as well. And in fact, when I was in the sales team at Moody's, I had a KYC specialist tell me sales and marketing is part of KYC because it's knowing your customer. So that was quite an interesting way of framing it. Very similar to what Ethio told us as well in the trade credit one I Want episode where she was like they've gone from order to cash checking credit after the fact to lead to cash of well let's actually pre vet who we want to send ourselves people to because we don't want to waste their time. Don't always potential customers time. So you can front load some of this stuff and it becomes part of your due diligence a lot earlier in the process these days. Absolutely. Activity for the sake of activity is not going to help anybody hit that target. So if you have a sales team that are speaking to businesses that you either can't close from a credit perspective, there are sanctions concerns or anything related to compliance. Ultimately that's just wasted time selling to an organization that you could ultimately do business with. So by bringing some of that stuff further up the process, we're really enabling companies to focus on deals they can close. Yeah, let's. You kind of did it before and you talked about second order effects but let's like walk it through. So missing the number is your biggest risk in business. Just run with that for now. So that's that top line revenue number. What actually happens like so the share price drops is one example that you gave. Sometimes depends on the story going on in the market. But then. Then what happens after that? What happens after that? How do you sort of think through that logically? Yeah. So if I think back to some of the experiences I've had working in this area over the last 10 years, I think one of the issues with hitting the number is the pointing of fingers internally within an organization. Everybody within an organization sort of points fingers at each other. And ultimately as an organization, for you to be successful and hit your target and to really be competitive in your market, you need to be harmonious organization. And by missing your target, ultimately that will drive less harmonious practices within the business and ultimately that compounds the issue even further. Yeah. And then you're back around the cycle. So instead of just looking at construction companies in the Northwest, by looking at a variety of different characteristics of the type of person you're trying to speak to, you can get a smaller list, but a smaller list of people who are more likely to buy or procure your services. And what we've seen in the last few years is that how that ICP is generated has started to develop more into the consumer model. Because there is a realization that the more specific you can get in the terms of the type of business you're trying to reach out to, the more success your team can have. Now to go back to your original question, sort of where is the general market? I do think it is a journey that organizations are on. Obviously the larger you are, the more money you have to invest in these new technologies and maintaining your data and enriching it with more content, the smaller you are, the more difficult it is to do that. So you know, startup organizations, for example, a lot of them are very CEO sales focused. But as organizations grow, especially those that are in sort of technology markets where there is more use of this type of thing, we definitely see more detail going into the go to market approach in terms of the number of teams, the amount of data they use to actually target and reach out to potential customers. Understood. So keep saying the word, right? Data, data analytics, data points, et cetera. It's all about the data once again. And ultimately what that means is that when you then go to market, you tell your marketing team to contact people and to invite them to events. So, AI within this space, obviously very interesting because driving to hit your number, as we've said, is one of the biggest risks, but it's also one of the biggest opportunities for a business. If you can greatly surpass where you said you were going to be. If you're listed, your stock price could significantly increase. If you're private, it unlocks more money for investment. So everybody is really interested in how using AI can help you exceed your sales targets. Now, one of the big challenges that we see within this space, going back to one of the points I made earlier, is that the data that people have on their customers is typically relatively poor and doesn't necessarily have a lot of characteristics about who those organizations are. And if you're working in an environment where the data you're putting into that AI is lacking or is poor, potentially all you're going to do is increase the momentum of poor decisions as opposed to making better decisions. So when we're often talking about, hey, how AI can be used within this space, the first point we always want to talk about is actually making sure the data that you're putting into that AI is as effective as possible. Because if you're going to be investing a lot of time and money into this, if you're going to be trusting something to be doing automated outreach to individuals like you on LinkedIn, you want to be confident that the information you're feeding it is trustworthy. And if you haven't solved that upfront, you might lead yourself to the point where you're creating a more. More activity, but not necessarily getting better results from using that technology. I mean, a brief, brief anecdote here, but I think hopefully it will set us up for the next question I wanted to ask you is you mentioned hard sell. Right.
